Paraguay.status: Fighting AUS for 2nd place. USA already has 6pts (safe). Win = lock 2nd. Draw probably enough on GD too.

Australia.status: Same as Paraguay — this is essentially a play-in for 2nd place qualification.

PAR.NOTE: Very low-possession, low-volume team (21.5%, 34.7% possession) but efficient — won GD2 1-0 on just 2 SOT. High card count came in the blowout loss (5), low in the disciplined win (2).

AUS.NOTE: Low-possession defensive-block team (28.4%, 38.1%) but direct/efficient on the counter — 0-1 offsides in both matches despite low possession. Classic 2+ offsides NO-family defensive underdog.

context_flags: VIRTUAL_PLAYOFF: PAR and AUS both on 3pts — this match decides 2nd place in the group. Both teams fully motivated, NOT a dead rubber.
HIGH_DRAW_PROB: Draw market at 40.3% (RULE8 territory) — expect a tight, low-event match.
AUS_OFFSIDE_NO_FAMILY: AUS is the defensive underdog (23.3%), 0-1 offsides in both WC matches despite low possession. 2+ offsides NO-family applies cleanly here.
PAR_LOW_VOLUME: Paraguay generates very few SOT (1, 2) but wins on efficiency — do not over-credit attacking props for Paraguay players.

CARDS_MARKET_VS_HISTORY_GAP: ESPN combined card average (PAR 3.5 + AUS 2.0) suggests higher card count than market implies (Over3.5=0.26). Resolved by noting both teams' high-card games were their LOSSES (frustration cards), and this match is genuinely competitive/high-stakes rather than a blowout — shading toward the market's lower, more calibrated number.

questions.text: Will Australia be caught offside 2 or more times?
questions.reasoning: AUS offsides: 1 (vs TUR), 0 (vs USA). λ=0.5 → Poisson P(≥2)=0.09. AUS is the defensive underdog again today (23.3%) — same 2+ offsides NO-family that has gone 10/10 across the tournament, including AUS's own GD2 match. Floor slightly above raw Poisson for tail risk.

questions.text: Will Australia commit more fouls than Paraguay?
questions.reasoning: PAR fouls avg 16.0, AUS avg 14.0 — history favors PAR fouling more (i.e. AUS NO). But Most-cards market has AUS (37.4%) essentially level with PAR (36.5%), conflicting signal. Shrink toward 0.45.

questions.text: Will Paraguay be caught offside 2 or more times?
questions.reasoning: PAR offsides: 1 (vs USA blowout loss), 3 (vs TUR disciplined win). λ=2.0 → Poisson P(≥2)=0.594. Paraguay is the favorite today (36%) and will press to win the group's 2nd-place play-in — not a defensive-underdog scenario, so the NO-family does not apply. Trust the raw rate.

questions.text: Will Australia have more shots on target than Paraguay in the second half?
questions.reasoning: Full-match SOT averages: AUS 3.0, PAR 1.5 — AUS generates 2x Paraguay's shot volume. Half-split Skellam (λ_AUS_2H=1.5, λ_PAR_2H=0.75) gives P(AUS>PAR)=0.55. Small sample (n=2 each) keeps confidence medium, not high.

questions.text: Will the second half have more total goals than the first half?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Highest-scoring-half, Second_Half = 35.78%. Trust the liquid market.
Smarkets FTR: Paraguay 35.99%. Direct tier-1 market, high draw probability (40.3%) reflects the genuinely competitive nature of this 2nd-place play-in. No reason to deviate.

questions.text: Will the second half have 2 or more total goals?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: 2H Over 1.5 goals = 27.5%. Consistent with the high draw probability and tight, low-event match expected.

questions.text: Will there be 4 or more total cards shown?
questions.reasoning: Market Over3.5 cards = 25.78% (internally consistent with Over1.5=76% and Over2.5=49% — clean decay curve implies mean ~2.7-3.0 cards). ESPN history average is higher but skewed by each team's blowout-loss games; this match is competitive, not a blowout, so shade toward the market.

questions.text: Will Julio Enciso have at least 1 shot on target?
questions.reasoning: Smarkets offer-only (illiquid) full-match SOT≥1 = 0.625, adjusted ×0.945 = 0.591. But Paraguay's TEAM SOT output has been very low (1, 2 per match) — even as the primary attacker, Enciso getting 1 SOT from a team generating only ~1.5 total is not as likely as the illiquid market suggests. Shade down.

strongest_picks: Q1: AUS 2+ offsides = 0.12 (HIGHEST CONFIDENCE — 10/10 NO-family, AUS defensive underdog again)
Q7: 2H has 2+ goals = 0.28 (HIGHEST CONFIDENCE — direct market, high draw probability confirms tight/low-event match)
Q5: 2H more goals than 1H = 0.36 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — direct market)

Q1_AUS_2plus_offsides.note: Biggest single win of the match. 2+ offsides NO-family for defensive underdogs now extends further — huge gap vs crowd (44%) who treated this like a coinflip.

Q3_PAR_2plus_offsides.note: We trusted Paraguay's raw offside rate (avg 2.0) believing they'd press for the 2nd-place spot, but the 0-0 result suggests both teams played a cagey, low-event, low-risk match rather than committed attacking football — the virtual-playoff stakes produced caution, not urgency.

Q4_AUS_more_2H_SOT.note: Same root cause as Q3 — 0-0 means a low-shot-volume, conservative match overall. Our Skellam model assumed normal attacking output; the actual game was unusually flat for both sides.

Q8_4plus_cards.note: Second-biggest win. Market-anchored read (illiquid Smarkets cards curve) beat the crowd's much higher 47% by a wide margin — a 0-0 cagey playoff match stayed disciplined, not chippy.

key_confirmations: Q1 AND Q8 both confirm: when we shade DOWN below an inflated crowd on a 'something happens' question and trust either the NO-offside family or a market-anchored low curve, we win big (combined +37.04 RBP from these two alone).

Q3 and Q4 (our two losses) share a root cause: we assumed a 'virtual playoff' would produce MORE attacking urgency. The actual match was a cagey 0-0 — high-stakes play-in matches between evenly-matched teams can suppress event volume rather than inflate it. Worth a new rule: HIGH_DRAW_PROB + similar-pts-teams => lean toward fewer events across ALL props, not just goals.
